---
title: "Computational Musicology"
format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
    toc: false
---

## About me

I am Stan Pieter van Kuik, a student at the University of Amsterdam. This page presents my portfolio for the course *Computational Musicology*.

## Portfolio background

Over the last couple of years, I have noticed that my listening behavior seems to be divided quite strongly by season, especially between summer and winter. I also tend to organize my favorite kinds of music into separate playlists, mainly dance music, hip hop, and chill music that I listen to in order to relax. This made me curious whether that seasonal pattern is actually reflected in the music itself, or whether it is mainly just a feeling.

For this portfolio, I built a corpus based on my own listening behavior. The corpus consists of six playlists: separate winter and summer playlists for dance, hip hop, and chill music. This creates a clear structure for comparison across both season and genre, while keeping the corpus closely connected to music that is personally meaningful to me.

To explore this corpus in a structured way, I will first examine the playlists at the level of individual tracks. This begins with a general corpus exploration using Spotify track-level features, after which I will look more closely at chroma features, loudness, timbre features, and temporal features. After these feature-specific analyses, I will return to the corpus as a whole through classification, regression, and clustering, before concluding with a broader reflection on what I learned from the analysis.

## Portfolio sections

::: {.card}
### Corpus Exploration
A first general inspection of the corpus using Spotify track-level features.

[Open page](corpus_exploration.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Chroma Features
Analysis of harmonic content and pitch-class distributions across the corpus.

[Open page](chroma_features.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Loudness
Exploration of loudness patterns and how they differ across season and genre.

[Open page](loudness.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Timbre Features
Analysis of timbral properties and broader sound-world differences within the corpus.

[Open page](timbre_features.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Temporal Features
Investigation of tempo, rhythm, and other temporal aspects of the playlists.

[Open page](temporal_features.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Classification / Regression
Using predictive methods to test whether playlist categories can be identified computationally.

[Open page](classification_regression.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Clustering
Exploring whether tracks group together naturally based on their musical features.

[Open page](clustering.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Conclusions
Final reflections on the corpus and what I learned from the analyses.

[Open page](conclusions.html)
:::

## Weekly assignments

::: {.card}
### Week 7: Computational Musicology and (Audio) Visualisation
(not available right now, will be added later)
:::

::: {.card}
### Week 8: Chroma Features
(not available right now, will be added later)
:::

::: {.card}
### Week 9: Timbre Features / Self-Similarity Matrices
(not available right now, will be added later)
:::

::: {.card}
### Week 10: Key and Chord Estimation
(not available right now, will be added later)
:::

::: {.card}
### Week 11: Novelty Functions and Tempograms
Analysis of *Afraid to Feel* by LF System

[Open page](week11.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### Week 12: Classification and Clustering
Clustering my Spotify 2025 Wrapped

[Open page](week12.html)
:::
